does: This paper proposes Graph Direct Preference Optimization (GraphDPO), a generalization of DPO that leverages the full preference structure in training data where multiple rollouts exist per prompt. Instead of collapsing rankings into independent pairwise comparisons, GraphDPO constructs directed acyclic graphs from rollout orderings where edges represent dominance relations. The method optimizes a graph-structured objective inspired by the Plackett-Luce model that aggregates supervision across graph neighborhoods, enforcing preference transitivity. The approach introduces an equivalence-class construction for handling discrete or sparse preference signals by grouping responses with identical preferences into graph layers. GraphDPO recovers standard pairwise DPO as a special case while reducing supervision conflicts and instability from redundant pairs.
connects: This work builds directly on Direct Preference Optimization, which simplified RLHF by using pairwise preference comparisons rather than full reward models. GraphDPO responds to a practical limitation of DPO: real training data often contains multiple outputs per prompt with natural ranking structure, but standard DPO discards this information by flattening rankings into independent pairs. The paper extends prior work on preference learning and ranking models, particularly drawing on the Plackett-Luce framework for modeling structured preferences. It also connects to broader literature on enforcing consistency constraints in learning-to-rank and preference modeling, addressing how transitivity violations arise from naive pair construction and how graph-based supervision can resolve conflicts in preference annotations.
enables: This work enables more efficient use of ranking data in language model alignment, potentially reducing data requirements and training instability. By properly exploiting the structure in multi-rollout settings, GraphDPO could lead to improved sample efficiency in preference learning. The framework opens directions for handling richer preference structures beyond rankings, such as partial orders or annotated preference sets. It also suggests future work on scaling graph-based preference optimization to large models and combining graph structures with other alignment approaches. The principled treatment of transitivity and consistency could influence how preference data is collected and processed in RLHF pipelines, and may extend to other domains where structured pairwise comparisons arise naturally.
